title: Penne alla boscaiola
subtitle: Sausage, porcini, and tomato — a forest keeper's pasta
excerpt: Boscaiola means 'woodsman's style': sausage, dried porcini, and tomato over penne. The porcini soaking liquid goes into the sauce, carrying a dark, fungal intensity that fresh mushrooms cannot match. Simple, robust, ready in half an hour.
sourceNotes: Pasta alla boscaiola is a Tuscan and Umbrian preparation, documented in regional Italian cookbooks from the late nineteenth century. Artusi's chapter on Tuscan meat sauces in La scienza in cucina (1891, public domain) covers the sausage-and-mushroom ragù family from which boscaiola descends. The dish varies by province: some versions add cream, others keep it tomato-only; Umbrian versions often include local black truffle.

recipe.freezeNotes: The sauce freezes well for up to 3 months. Freeze before adding the pasta.
recipe.batchNotes: Double the sauce and freeze half for another meal.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Sauce keeps in the fridge for 3 days.

glossaryTerms.definition: Boletus edulis, the cep or penny bun mushroom. Dried porcini are a concentrated flavour base, used in Italian cooking to add deep, savoury intensity. The soaking liquid carries much of the flavour and should be used in the sauce.

content.text: The name comes from the Italian word for a forest keeper, and the dish tastes like one. Italian pork sausage, dried
, tomato, and a splash of wine: it is a ragù that takes 25 minutes rather than two hours. The porcini soaking liquid, dark brown and intensely savoury, goes into the sauce — never down the drain.

Squeeze the sausage meat out of the casings. You want it loose, not in rounds, so it breaks into small pieces and coats the pasta. Italian-style pork sausages (mildly seasoned with fennel) work best. Plain British pork sausages are a workable substitute.

items.prepNote: soaked in 200 ml hot water for 20 minutes

content.text: Cover the {{mushrooms-porcini-dried}} of dried porcini with 200 ml of just-boiled water and leave for 20 minutes. Lift the porcini out, squeeze them gently, and chop roughly. Pour the soaking liquid through a fine sieve, leaving the grit behind. You need 150 ml of clear liquid; top up with water if short.

Warm the {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide pan over medium-high heat. Add the sausage meat in walnut-sized pieces. Press down lightly and leave for 2 minutes until well browned on the base. Break up with a spoon and cook for 2 more minutes until cooked through. Remove the sausage with a slotted spoon and set aside.

In the same pan over medium heat, add the {{onion}} onion. Cook for 5 minutes until softened. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and the chopped porcini. Cook for 2 minutes. Pour in the {{white-wine-dry}} of white wine and let it bubble for 1 minute. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tomatoes and the strained porcini liquid. Return the sausage to the pan. Simmer for 12 minutes until the sauce thickens.

Cook the {{pasta-dried}} of penne in generously salted boiling water until al dente. Drain and toss through the sauce with a splash of pasta water. Serve with the {{parmesan}} of Parmesan and the chopped parsley if using.

items.cause: Porcini soaking liquid not strained, or poured too fast carrying the sediment
items.fix: Always strain through a fine-mesh sieve; pour slowly, leaving the last 2 cm in the bowl

items.cause: Not browned first, or added to the sauce before colouring
items.fix: Brown in a hot pan before adding liquid; direct heat on the meat first is what develops flavour
The liquid is the most flavourful part; always use it; soak for at least 20 minutes
Simmer without a lid for the full 12 minutes; the sauce should coat a spoon

content.text: Boscaiola belongs to the wooded interior of central Italy (Tuscany, Umbria, and the Apennine foothills), where foraging for fungi is a serious autumn pursuit and pork is cured and eaten in every imaginable form. The dish does not appear in coastal or southern Italian cooking, which tends to be lighter and less reliant on dried mushrooms. In Umbria, where black truffle grows abundantly, boscaiola sometimes becomes a vehicle for shaved tartufo nero; elsewhere it stays simple.
